docs.title: Aetherion
docs.text: A homebrew Pathfinder 2e setting. This site collects everything the players know — the campaign primer, the public world lore, the nations, the pantheon, and more.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• Player's Guide — the campaign-specific guide for character creation, themes, tone, and table expectations. Read this first.
  • The World You Know — what every Skyborn knows about the world. The in-world primer.

docs.title: Browse the world
docs.text: Use the navigation tree on the left, or the search bar above.

docs.title: About this site
docs.text: This is a curated, player-facing slice of the full Aetherion design notes. GM-only material is held back; the world reveals itself in play.

docs.title: The Antiquarian
docs.text: An old shopkeeper in Aurion's mid-quarter. Collector and dealer in old objects, scholar of vanished religions, weirdly well-informed about ruins, relics, and obscure history. He pays well for relic-recovery work and employs intermediaries to handle the field jobs. Business associates call him the Antiquarian; those who know him better call him Yverin.

docs.title: Meeting him
docs.text:
• Wry, patient, a little sad.
  • Pays well and pays on time.
  • Knows more than he ought to about places and things he has no obvious connection to.
  • Won't say what he was before he was the Antiquarian.
  • His name "Yverin" doesn't quite fit any culture's naming convention. Most people who notice assume it's an old family form or a scholarly affectation.

docs.title: Aetherion — Player's Guide
docs.text:
Welcome to Aetherion, a homebrew Pathfinder 2e campaign. You'll be playing levels 1 through 20 across (we expect) a multi-year run, Saturdays 6-10pm. This guide is for you to read before the campaign starts so you can build a character that fits.

For the in-world primer — what every Skyborn knows about the continent, the Pantheon, the Pit, and the nations — see The World You Know. This guide focuses on this campaign specifically: the shape of the story, how you're connected, and what kinds of characters will thrive.

docs.title: What this campaign is
docs.text:
A 1-20 epic in the home setting your GM has been building. Some shape notes:

  • Anthology at the start; cohesive at the end. Early sessions are mostly self-contained jobs — recover a thing, escort a person, investigate a body. By the middle of the campaign, threads will start braiding into something larger. By the end, you'll be on the kind of quest that L1 characters would have been laughed at for accepting.
  • Stakes that grow with you. You won't start L1 trying to save the world. You'll be on that kind of quest by L20, but you'll have earned it.
  • Plot you can see. Your GM has a story he wants to tell. He's not going to railroad you, but the tracks are there and visible. Take hooks; follow plot threads. If you want to go somewhere unexpected, say so — the GM will pivot.
  • Mystery as content, not as gameplay. You don't have to solve a puzzle to advance the story. The world reveals itself as you play.

This is not a Kingmaker-style sandbox or a kingdom-building game. There will be some sandbox stretches, but the overall shape is "growing into a story" rather than "building a place."

docs.title: What this campaign is about
docs.text:
Themes you'll encounter, in case they help you pick a character:

  • Working for someone whose motives aren't entirely clear, and choosing to trust them anyway
  • The gap between official histories and what the records actually say
  • Communities of outsiders carving out lives in a world that wasn't built for them
  • The cost of mercy and the cost of cruelty
  • Becoming powerful enough to matter to gods

If you're drawn to characters who chafe against authority, who ask questions, or who quietly take care of people the world has forgotten — there's room for you here. If you'd rather play a true believer who slowly has to reckon with what they were taught — there's room for you too.

docs.title: Where the campaign starts
docs.text:
You all live or work in Aurion, capital of Highcrown — the political and religious center of Aetherion. The Skyward Inquisition headquarters here; so does the Majestal Court. It's a city of marble, smoke, scripture, and the loud whisper of orthodoxy.

You've each ended up taking work from a quirky shopkeeper named Yverin, who calls himself the Antiquarian. He runs a mid-district shop full of old relics, older books, and what most people would call useless dust. He pays well for small jobs — recoveries, deliveries, the occasional discreet errand. He's widely considered eccentric but harmless. He's old. He's wry. He talks too much about ancient history and not enough about himself.

You don't ask too many questions. The pay is good.

docs.title: Why you're together
docs.text:
The group's agreed framing: you work for the same private patron — Yverin. Beyond that, you decide your relationships. Were you hired together? Hired separately and met on the job? Old friends who answered the same ad? Strangers who learned to be useful to each other? Up to you.

Some questions to think about for your character:

  • How did Yverin find you, or how did you find him?
  • What did he offer that you needed?
  • Why do you keep coming back?

docs.title: Basics
docs.text:
• Starting level: 1
  • Ancestry: any PF2e ancestry your GM approves (see notes below)
  • Class: any PF2e class (see notes below)
  • Background: standard PF2e backgrounds work; campaign-specific backgrounds are in development (see below)
  • Ability scores: standard PF2e creation
  • Hit points & equipment: standard

docs.title: Ancestries — what's common
docs.text:
The five Skyborn ancestries dominate Aetherion society. Each has a "home" nation and a cultural reputation, though plenty of people live outside their home:

  • Human — Highcrown majority; politically and religiously dominant; the "default" ancestry of the continent's power structures.
  • Elf — Sylvaris majority; scholarly, long-lived, somewhat insular; lots of contact with the Archivists of Moonbough.
  • Dwarf — Ankarum majority; fortress culture, formal, structural engineering; the Keelwrights literally hold the continent together.
  • Halfling — Tumblebrook majority; agricultural, communal, gentle; feeds the rest of Aetherion.
  • Gnome — Glymswick majority; inventive, irreverent, mechanical; the Gearwright Consortium runs Crowngear and the drift control that keeps Aetherion mobile.

docs.title: Ancestries — versatile heritages
docs.text: Half-elves, half-orcs, and tieflings all exist in Aetherion. They're more common than polite society admits. The Skyward Inquisition gets twitchy about visible "hybrids" but rarely acts on it openly in cosmopolitan Aurion. If you want to play a character with an unusual heritage — a striking visual feature, a partial lineage, something the world doesn't have a name for — talk to the GM; we'll find a way to make it work.

docs.title: Ancestries — uncommon and rare
docs.text:
• Awakened animal: Exists, but extremely rare in Aetherion. The Inquisition treats awakened animals with suspicion. If you want to play one, talk to the GM — there's a campaign-specific path for this concept that gives your character an interesting place in the world.
  • Other Lost Omens ancestries: Generally available with GM approval. Some will fit Aetherion cleanly; some will need a conversation.

docs.title: Classes — what fits where
docs.text:
Aetherion is friendly to almost every PF2e class. Some notes that might shape your concept:

Divine and faith-based - Cleric / Champion of the Twelve — pillars of Skyborn society. Public, respected, and watched by the Inquisition. Lots of mechanical and narrative support. - Cleric / Champion of an unsanctioned power — technically possible, deeply unwise. Don't pick this without a long conversation. - Oracle — exists but draws attention. Strange visions are often diagnosed as illness or possession by orthodox priests.

Arcane - Wizard / Magus — the Sylvari academies are the seat of formal arcane study, but practitioners exist across all five nations. - Witch — exists; eccentric patrons raise eyebrows. - Sorcerer — common; bloodline backgrounds work as normal.

Primal and occult - Druid — exists, but Skyborn druidic traditions favor sky and storm and wind over deep-forest nature. - Bard — beloved everywhere; the festival circuits in Tumblebrook and the court masques in Aurion both want them. - Psychic — uncommon, mystical, draws attention from the Inquisition.

Martial - Fighter / Barbarian / Ranger — common in all nations. - Rogue / Investigator — Aurion has more shadow than light. Plenty of room. - Monk — uncommon, considered exotic; some Sylvari traditions support it. - Swashbuckler / Gunslinger — both fit; gunslingers especially work for Glymswick characters or Cogbarrow-trained ones.

Newer classes - Kineticist — rare but accepted. An esoteric tradition. Aetherion mythologizes air, water, metal, and wood more than earth and fire, but all elements exist. - Alchemist — common in Glymswick; the Crowngear Consortium loves an alchemist. - Magus / Thaumaturge / Inventor / Summoner — all available; expect some side-eye from the orthodoxy depending on flavor. - Commander — Khalvar Hold's strategists; Ankarum produces a lot of these. - Guardian — common across the five nations; "stand in front, take the hit" is a respected role.

If a class isn't on this list, it's still probably available — ask.

docs.title: Backgrounds
docs.text:
All standard PF2e backgrounds work. We're also developing a few campaign-specific backgrounds that will be available before the campaign starts. Previews:

  • Shop Apprentice — you've worked at Yverin's antique shop for at least a year. You know where things are kept and which customers to be wary of.
  • Skyfall Survivor — you were caught in a Skyfall event as a child and lived. The experience left you... attuned to certain stones.
  • Inquisition Castaway — you were once a sworn Skyward Inquisitor or initiate, and left. You know how they work, and they know you.
  • Tether's End Native — you grew up in the descent town, on the only point of contact between the continent and... whatever is below. You've seen things no other Skyborn has.
  • Failed Apprentice — Crowngear, the Sylvari academies, or one of the noble houses' private schools — you washed out. You know the systems from the inside.

Mechanical details for these are still being drafted. If one calls to you, pick it as a "placeholder" and we'll finalize together.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Skills that pay off across the campaign:

  • Society — political, factional, and historical knowledge. Will be useful constantly.
  • Religion — public theology of the Twelve, the calendar, the iconography.
  • Arcana / Occultism — shards and weird relics show up a lot.
  • Stealth / Thievery / Deception — Yverin's work is not always above-board.
  • Diplomacy — for talking to fences, nobles, scholars, and the occasional Inquisitor.

Useful but not central:

  • Athletics — always pays off in combat-heavy sessions.
  • Survival — most of the campaign starts urban but the wilderness pays off later.
  • Nature — strong for druids/rangers; less central than Religion/Society for most.
  • Performance — surprisingly useful for an Aurion-set early game.

docs.title: Lore skills worth picking up
docs.text:
If your background gives you a lore slot or you want to grab one with a skill increase:

  • Aurion Lore — the home city; streets, factions, neighborhoods.
  • Inquisition Lore — how they operate, who their senior figures are.
  • Skyfall Lore — the falling-relic phenomenon and its folklore.
  • Crowngear Lore — Glymswick technology, drift control, mechanical artifice.
  • Sky Travel Lore — bridges, ropeways, caravans, airships.

docs.title: Useful feats to consider
docs.text:
This is suggestion, not prescription. Feats that tend to pay off in Aetherion's early sessions:

  • Streetwise / Connections / Group Impression — for navigating Aurion's social fabric.
  • Pickpocket / Subtle Theft — Yverin's errands often shade gray.
  • Recognize Spell / Trick Magic Item — shards and relics are everywhere.
  • Quick Identification / Assurance (Religion or Society) — knowing what's in front of you fast.
  • Diehard / Toughness — Aurion has more violence than its facade suggests.

docs.title: Shards (campaign-specific mechanic)
docs.text:
You're going to find shards. A lot of them, eventually. A few things to know:

  • Shards are bits of crystallized... something. Old, valuable, weird. They sometimes whisper. They sometimes show wielders flashes of memory that aren't their own.
  • Mechanically: PF2e relics. If you've used relic rules before, that's the framework.
  • No corruption track. Bonding with a shard won't make your character evil or possessed. The pressure is purely narrative.
  • You'll likely bond with one over the campaign. Voluntary. Reversible until very late.
  • More detail at first session. For now: don't avoid shards out of habit, and don't grab them as expected loot either. They have weight.

docs.title: Table expectations
docs.text:
What we ask of you:

  • One character for the campaign. If your character dies, we build a new one together. (Not the focus of this campaign, though — character death will be rare in the published-AP sense.)
  • Show up if you can. We meet weekly. Missing a session is fine; the story is built to tolerate it, and you won't lose your seat or critical story moments.
  • Engage with the world. You don't have to be a master roleplayer. You don't have to do voices. But talk to named NPCs, follow plot hooks you can see, and let the story land.
  • Tell us what you want. If a thread isn't working for you, say so. If you want more focus on something — a class feature, a relationship, a backstory thread — ask. The GM is happy to pivot.
  • Bring a backstory. Even one paragraph is enough. Give the GM something to grab. The earlier you can share it, the more of it can show up in play.

docs.title: Pacing
docs.text:
• Saturday 6-10pm, weekly. Same slot as Kingmaker.
  • This campaign starts after Kingmaker concludes. No firm date yet; track the Nyrissa arc.
  • Roughly 1 level per 4-6 sessions, varying by adventure shape.
  • Multi-year run. Plan accordingly. Build a character you'll still want to play in two years.

docs.title: Backstory prompts
docs.text:
Use as much or as little as helps. Even just a few sentences for each is great.

  1. How did Yverin find you, or how did you find him?
  2. What does he pay you for? What kind of work do you do for him?
  3. What's your relationship to Aurion? Native? Recent arrival? Returning after years away?
  4. What's your relationship to the Twelve? Devout? Lapsed? Cynical? Fearful? Curious?
  5. What's your relationship to the Inquisition? Awe? Distrust? Personal grudge? Indifference?
  6. What do you want? Money? Knowledge? Family? Vengeance? To be left alone? To matter to someone?
  7. What's something other characters would notice about you on first meeting? A scar, a tic, a turn of phrase, an unusual possession.

You don't need to answer all of these. Pick what helps. The GM will work with whatever you give him.

docs.title: Open questions / what's still in flux
docs.text:
This guide is a working draft — it'll evolve as we approach session one. Things still being firmed up:

  • The home nation. Aurion / Highcrown is the working assumption (it's where Yverin's shop is). Talk to the GM if you wanted to build a character from elsewhere with a strong reason to be in Aurion.
  • Final mechanical drafts of the campaign-specific backgrounds. Coming before session 1.
  • Awakened-animal path. Talk to the GM if this concept calls to you.
  • Any rule we should house-rule. If there's a PF2e rule you've found wonky, raise it now.

docs.title: Asking questions
docs.text:
Ask the GM anytime. There are no dumb questions — especially during character creation. The sooner we hear what you're building, the more we can integrate your character into the campaign's opening.

Welcome to Aetherion. The shop is open.

docs.title: The World You Know
docs.text: What every Skyborn knows about the world they live in, without having to roll for it. The story you were taught growing up — by your parents, by your priests, by the festival sermons and the children's rhymes.

docs.title: The world above
docs.text:
You live on Aetherion, the great floating continent suspended in the sky. It has forests, mountains, rivers, farms, cities, and weather, much like any land — but it is held above the world by divine grace and the engineering of the dwarves and gnomes. Most of you will never visit any land that is not Aetherion.

There are five peoples — Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling, Gnome — each with its own nation and place in the harmony of things.

docs.title: The Twelve
docs.text:
Twelve gods watch over Aetherion: Maelstrom, Memory, Mercy, Malice, Majesty, Madness, Mirth, Melancholy, Mortis, Motive, Muse, Might. Together they are the Twelve or the Pantheon.

Some say they are real beings who walk among us. Some say they are aspects of one greater divinity. Some say they are conceptual principles. The Court teaches all three views without contradiction — choose your own, or hold them all.

Majesty is most often seen and is recognized as the publicly-leading god. The others appear more rarely, in places appropriate to their aspect.

docs.title: The Vs — the Fallen Powers
docs.text: The Vs were gods who once tried to drag Aetherion down. The Twelve defeated them. They are now confined to the Underworld. To invoke them is heresy.

docs.title: The Pit below
docs.text:
Below Aetherion lies the Pit — a hellscape of corruption, poisoned air, twisted creatures, and demonic powers. Its inhabitants ("Groundborn") are tainted and dangerous. Descent is forbidden. The Anchor exists only to hold Aetherion steady, never to invite passage.

The children's rhyme — The Pit Below — is the version most of you learned as kids.

docs.title: Skyfall
docs.text:
The Twelve sometimes scatter fragments of divine light across the heavens — Skyshards — which fall to Aetherion as tests of worthiness. The pure may wield them; the unworthy are destroyed. Today, Skyfalls are rare.

Cultural names differ: - Dwarves call them World-Bones. - Elves call them Starfruit or Moon-amber. - Humans call them Relics.

docs.title: The Moon and Her Sister
docs.text: The moon was once whole, until her jealous sister rose from the deep night and struck her. The clash scattered the sister's bones into the planetary ring above us. The scar on the moon is her wound. Priests call the scar the Eye of Judgment and the ring the Halo of Aetherion.

docs.title: Travel
docs.text: Most of you have never been in a flying vehicle. Skybridges, ropeways, caravans, and river boats run by the halfling League of Feathered Roads move people and goods across Aetherion. The military fly airships, and a few rich nobles or gnomish industrialists have personal craft, but ordinary people walk and ride.

docs.title: The nations, briefly
docs.text:
• Highcrown — Aurion (Human): the political and religious center. The Majestal Court interprets the will of the Twelve from here.
  • Sylvaris — Moonbough (Elf): luminous trees, suspended gardens, observatories. The largest surviving library in the world is here.
  • Ankarum — Khalvar Hold (Dwarf): the Keelwrights keep Aetherion structurally whole. Runic engineering, mining, mountains.
  • Glymswick — Cogbarrow (Gnome): invention, drift control. The Gearwright's Consortium keeps Aetherion moving.
  • Tumblebrook — Greenmere (Halfling): the breadbasket. Farms, pastures, festivals, and the trade roads.
  • Tether's End: the ramshackle village around the Grand Anchor. Smugglers, outcasts, exiles. Most respectable people stay away.

docs.title: The Public Story
docs.text: This is the cosmology as taught on Aetherion. Most Skyborn believe it sincerely.

docs.title: The Twelve
docs.text:
Twelve gods watch over Aetherion from the heavens. Their names are aspects of the human condition: Maelstrom, Memory, Mercy, Malice, Majesty, Madness, Mirth, Melancholy, Mortis, Motive, Muse, Might. They are worshipped collectively as the Twelve or the Pantheon; individual aspect veneration is supplementary.

Three sanctioned views of who/what they are coexist among the faithful — active beings, aspects of a single divinity, or conceptual principles.

docs.title: The Fallen Vs
docs.text: The Vs are the Fallen Powers — gods who once sought to drag Aetherion down into ruin. Defeated by the Twelve, they are now confined to the corruption of the Underworld. To invoke them is heresy. See world/religion/inquisition-policy.md.

docs.title: Skyfall
docs.text:
The Twelve sometimes scatter fragments of divine light across the heavens — Skyshards — that fall to Aetherion as tests of worthiness. The pure may wield them; the unworthy are destroyed. The corrupt below would defile them; the worthy above are entrusted with stewardship.

Skyfalls were frequent centuries ago. Today they are rare.

For the cultural elaborations around shards — the cultural names, the rumored properties — see world/propaganda-and-folklore/shard-folklore.md.

docs.title: The Underworld
docs.text:
Below Aetherion lies the Pit — a hellscape of corruption, poisoned air, twisted creatures, and demonic powers. Its inhabitants ("Groundborn") are tainted: skin marked, eyes wrong, bodies warped. Descent is heresy. Trade with Groundborn is treason. The Anchor exists to hold Aetherion steady, never to invite passage.

The full Majestal Court sermon on this subject is in world/propaganda-and-folklore/inquisition-warnings.md.

docs.title: The Moon and Her Sister
docs.text:
Folk version: there was once a single shining moon. Her jealous sister rose from the deep night and hurled herself upon her. The clash shattered the sky, scattering the sister's bones into a crown of stars around the world (the ring). The scar on the moon is her wound, weeping light into the dark.

Priests' version: the scar is the Eye of Judgment — watching mortals. The ring is the Halo of Aetherion — proof the gods defend the world against chaos. Some sects claim each fragment is a shard of angelic essence.

Both versions in narrative form: world/propaganda-and-folklore/moon-mythology.md.

docs.title: The Races
docs.text: The five Aetherion ancestries are the chosen peoples, raised to civilization under the protection of the Twelve. The "monstrous" races of the Pit are corruption made flesh.

docs.title: Factions
docs.text: Organizations and political bodies of Aetherion, organized by region of influence.

docs.title: Highcrown
docs.text:
• Majestal Court — theocratic ruling body.
  • Skyward Inquisition — secret police.
  • Order of the Pure Flame — militant zealots, shard hunters.
  • Crown Assembly — noble class.
  • Skyguard — standing army.
  • Cloud Knights of Aurion — elite knightly order.
  • Bank of Aurion — financial power; secret shard hoarder.

docs.title: Sylvaris
docs.text:
• Sylvari Council of Boughs — elven confederation.
  • Archivists of Moonbough — lorekeepers.

docs.title: Ankarum
docs.text:
• Keelwrights of Ankarum — engineering guild; structural stability.
  • Black Anvil — heretic cult, looking for shard-forges.
  • Ember Faith — splinter sect awaiting the Red Ember's return.

docs.title: Glymswick
docs.text: • Gearwright's Consortium — gnomish syndicate; propulsion, drift, Crowngear.

docs.title: Tumblebrook
docs.text:
• Halfling Tithe League — food distribution.
  • League of Feathered Roads — caravan and skybridge network.

docs.title: Tether's End
docs.text:
• Salvage Syndicate — smugglers between Sky and Underworld.
  • Riftwatch — Anchor guards, mostly bribed.

docs.title: Cross-nation
docs.text:
• Veil Folk — heretic scholars; mostly correct.
  • Aetheric Collegium — sanctioned shard-scholars.
  • Lanternbearers — outlawed shard mystics.

docs.title: Black Anvil
docs.text: Dwarven heretic cult. Believe Aetherion's mountain rings hide deeper secrets — ancient caverns of original shard-forges.

docs.title: Ember Faith
docs.text: Dwarven splinter sect. Worship the lost Red Ember. Await its bearer's return.

docs.title: Keelwrights of Ankarum
docs.text: Dwarven engineering guild. Maintain Aetherion's structural stability — runic engineering, stabilizing chambers, skyquake response. Control mountain access, mining, and excavation. Monopolize rune-craft through strict guild licensing.

docs.title: Aetheric Collegium
docs.text: Human/gnomish magisters. Shard-phenomenon scholars under Majestal Court oversight.

docs.title: Lanternbearers
docs.text: Outlawed mystics. Hold shard rituals in secret caves. Believe Skyfall signals a higher truth the gods suppress.

docs.title: Veil Folk
docs.text: Heretic scholars. Believe the truth of Skyfall is hidden by the Majestal Court.

docs.title: Gearwright's Consortium
docs.text: Gnomish syndicate. Runs propulsion, the Crowngear, and drift mechanics via patents, sabotage, and trade cartels.

docs.title: Bank of Aurion
docs.text: Holds noble debts, funds wars. Secretly hoards shards to back its "faith coin."

docs.title: Cloud Knights of Aurion
docs.text: Elite knightly order, sworn to Majesty and throne. Operate Aetherion's premier airships.

docs.title: Crown Assembly
docs.text: Noble class. Alliances, assassinations, shard-bribery. Most houses hold minor shards.

docs.title: Majestal Court
docs.text: Theocratic ruling body. Interprets the will of the Twelve. Controls orthodoxy via sermons, festivals, inquisitions, censorship.

docs.title: Order of the Pure Flame
docs.text: Militant zealots. Guard shard vaults; lead shard-hunts; pursue unauthorized shard-bearers as crusaders.

docs.title: Skyguard
docs.text: Highcrown's standing army. "Defenders of purity." Operates airships and conventional forces.

docs.title: Skyward Inquisition
docs.text: Secret police. Hunts heresy, Ground-sympathy, forbidden shard lore. Disappearances, informants, closed trials. Feared but seen as protective by most citizens.

docs.title: Archivists of Moonbough
docs.text: Elven lorekeepers. Claim unbroken record from before the Skyborn era.

docs.title: Sylvari Council of Boughs
docs.text: Elven confederation. Loose alliance of houses — ostensibly united, persistently divided. Envoys, manipulation, forest-borne magic.

docs.title: Riftwatch
docs.text: Tether's End guards. Officially protect the Grand Anchor. Often bribed.

docs.title: Salvage Syndicate
docs.text: Tether's End smugglers. Move shards, goods, and people between Sky and Underworld.

docs.title: Halfling Tithe League
docs.text: Farmer cooperatives. Quietly manage food distribution across Aetherion.

docs.title: League of Feathered Roads
docs.text: Halfling caravan guild. Runs the continent's everyday transit infrastructure: skybridges, ropeways, overland caravans, river boats. Most Skyborn live and die on this network.

docs.text: The shape of the world as it exists today.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• aetherion-continent.md — the floating continent itself: drift, terrain, the Crowngear.
  • grand-anchor.md — the ancient black-metal structure that moors the continent at Tether's End.
  • tethers-end.md — the ramshackle village around the Anchor; the only semi-legal route down.
  • travel-and-flight.md — civilian, elite, military, and illicit transit across the continent.

docs.title: Geography & Drift
docs.text:
Aetherion is a massive floating continent suspended in the sky. It is stable enough that most inhabitants treat it as the entire civilized world. It has forests, mountains, rivers, farms, ruins, cities, and weather.

It is not naturally stable. It drifts on "chosen currents" of high-altitude wind and arcane resonance, nudged by colossal gyres and rune-engines. The gnomes' great machine — the Crowngear, beneath Cogbarrow — allows them to angle Aetherion's drift by subtle degrees. Sabotaging the Crowngear risks sending the continent into the forbidden ground.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• grand-anchor.md — the mooring structure.
  • travel-and-flight.md — how people move across it.
  • world/nations/_overview.md — the five nations spread across it.

docs.title: The Grand Anchor
docs.text: When Aetherion pauses its drift, it moors at one place: the Grand Anchor at Tether's End. The Anchor is a vast structure of black metal that does not rust, etched with symbols no scholar recognizes. It is older than the dwarves' runes, older than the gnomes' turbines. No one may approach without royal license; the bribed guards look away.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• tethers-end.md — the village around the Anchor.
  • world/factions/tethers-end/riftwatch.md — the guards.

docs.title: Tether's End
docs.text:
A ramshackle, creaking village lashed together around the Grand Anchor itself, clinging to cliffside ledges and rope-bridges, literally hanging off the side of the continent. Inhabited by outcasts, smugglers, thrill-seekers, anchor-workers, failed pilgrims, and exiles. The Salvage Syndicate operates here. So do the Riftwatch — officially the Anchor guards, practically the smugglers' partners.

Tether's End is the only semi-legal route toward the Pit. It is where Aetherion's biggest secrets are most accessible to mortals.

docs.title: Cultural status
docs.text: Treated as a sixth cultural pole of Aetherion despite being a ramshackle settlement rather than a nation. Inhabited by people the Skyborn cosmology has no place for — and therefore one of the campaign's most fertile locations for cross-cosmology contact, smuggled truth, and characters whose backstories don't fit anywhere else.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• grand-anchor.md — the structure the village hangs from.
  • the-pit.md — what lies below.
  • world/factions/tethers-end/_README.md — local factions.

docs.title: The Pit (The Surface)
docs.text:
Below Aetherion lies the Pit — a hellscape of corruption, poisoned air, twisted creatures, and demonic powers. Its inhabitants ("Groundborn") are tainted: skin marked, eyes wrong, bodies warped. Descent is heresy. Trade with Groundborn is treason. The Anchor exists to hold Aetherion steady, never to invite passage.

The folk and priestly material on the Pit is in world/propaganda-and-folklore/ (children's rhyme, sermons, warnings).

docs.title: How descent works
docs.text: See travel-and-flight.md — the three-gate descent system (theological taboo + physical danger + Tether's End bottleneck) makes descent committal. Almost no one goes down. Almost no one comes back.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/peoples/groundborn.md — the people who live there.
  • world/pantheons/seven/_overview.md — the surface's divine warlords.
  • world/history/the-conquest.md — how the current state of the surface came to be.

docs.title: Travel and Flight
docs.text:
Flight technology exists on Aetherion. Gnomish turbines, gravitic regulators, and the engineering that moves the continent itself can be miniaturized into personal or industrial-scale craft. But personal flight is not a civilian culture. The tech is expensive, specialized, and historically locked to industrial, military, and elite use. Ordinary citizens travel overland.

  • Civilian: the League of Feathered Roads — halfling caravan guild — runs the continent's everyday transit infrastructure. Skybridges between cities, ropeways through canyons, overland caravans, river boats. Most Skyborn live and die on this network.
  • Elite / specialized: small private flying craft exist for Highcrown nobles, gnomish industrialists, and a handful of mercantile elites. Visible but rare.
  • Military: Aetherion's military fields airships. Most citizens have seen them but never been aboard. The Skyguard and Cloud Knights of Aurion operate these.
  • Illicit: smuggler gliders and rigged contraptions exist, mostly around Tether's End. Dangerous, unreliable, and a hanging offense if caught using one for descent.

docs.title: Descent — The Pit Gate
docs.text:
Descent to the surface is gated at three layers:

  1. Theological and legal taboo. Descent is heresy. The Inquisition actively suppresses descent culture; the public is raised with sincere Pit-fear from the children's rhyme on up.
  2. Physical danger of the descent zone. The airspace between Aetherion and the surface is hostile: ongoing bombardment debris, atmospheric instability around the Anchor's resonance, shard interference, and V surveillance from below.
  3. Infrastructural bottleneck at the Grand Anchor. Tether's End is the only stabilized, predictable route down. Lifts, chains, smuggler routes — established but tightly controlled.

Descent is therefore committal. Anyone who goes down knows it's hard to come back up quickly. Almost no one tries.

docs.title: The War of the Three Shards
docs.text: 3rd century A.S. — roughly 2,700 years ago.

docs.title: Public version
docs.text:
In a single season, three burning stars fell: the Blue Flame (Sylvaris), the Red Ember (Ankarum), and the Golden Crown (Highcrown). Each was unmistakably significant.

The nations went to war over them. After a decade of bloodshed:

  • Blue Flame: shattered. Fragments lost.
  • Red Ember: vanished in a dwarven collapse. The Ember Faith awaits its bearer's return.
  • Golden Crown: sealed by Highcrown, "by the will of Majesty," never displayed again.

After the war, the Majestal Court declared open shard-war heretical.

"If three shards fall again, the skies themselves will burn."

docs.title: The Five Nations
docs.text:
The five Skyborn nations and their capitals.

Nation Capital Ancestry Theme Highcrown Aurion Human Theocratic central authority Sylvaris Moonbough Elf Memory, moonlight, longevity Ankarum Khalvar Hold Dwarf Structural stewardship, rune-craft Glymswick Cogbarrow Gnome Invention, drift control Tumblebrook Greenmere Halfling Pastoral, breadbasket, hidden networks

docs.title: Tether's End
docs.text: Treated as a sixth cultural pole despite being a ramshackle settlement rather than a nation — see world/geography/tethers-end.md.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/peoples/_overview.md — the Skyborn ancestries themselves.
  • world/factions/_overview.md — organizations operating within and across these nations.

docs.title: Ankarum (Dwarf)
docs.text:
Capital: Khalvar Hold. Dwarven for "the Mountain-Keeper's Hold."

The Keelwrights of Ankarum maintain Aetherion's structural stability — runic engineering, stabilizing chambers, skyquake response. They control mountain access, mining, and excavation. They monopolize rune-craft through strict guild licensing.

The Black Anvil is a dwarven heretic cult that believes Aetherion's mountain rings hide deeper secrets — ancient caverns of original shard-forges. They are widely dismissed as cranks.

The Ember Faith is a splinter sect awaiting the return of the Red Ember (the dwarven sacred relic that vanished in the collapse).

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/peoples/skyborn/dwarves.md — the dwarves of Ankarum.
  • world/factions/ankarum/_README.md — Ankarum-based factions.
  • world/history/war-of-the-three-shards.md — the Red Ember's loss.

docs.title: Khalvar Hold
docs.text:
Capital of Ankarum. In the old dwarven tongue, the name means "the Mountain-Keeper's Hold."

The structural-engineering heart of Aetherion. Keelwrights of Ankarum maintain their Stabilizing Halls beneath the Hold; the runic engineering that keeps Aetherion intact originates here. Dwarven nobility, master rune-smiths, and the senior councils of the engineering guilds live in vaulted halls cut into the surrounding mountains.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• ankarum.md — the nation.
  • world/peoples/skyborn/dwarves.md — the dwarves of Ankarum (including their naming conventions).
  • world/factions/ankarum/keelwrights.md — the engineering guild seated here.

docs.title: Glymswick (Gnome)
docs.text:
Capital: Cogbarrow.

If Ankarum keeps Aetherion whole, Glymswick keeps it moving. The gnomes' wind-harnessers, arcane turbines, and gravitic regulators control the continent's drift. Their crowning project is the Crowngear — see cogbarrow.md.

The Gearwright's Consortium runs propulsion, the Crowngear, and drift mechanics via patents, sabotage, and trade cartels.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/peoples/skyborn/gnomes.md — the gnomes of Glymswick.
  • world/geography/aetherion-continent.md — the continent the Crowngear steers.

docs.title: Highcrown (Human)
docs.text:
Capital: Aurion.

The political and religious center of Aetherion. The Majestal Court interprets the will of the Twelve; the Crown Assembly jockeys beneath it; the Skyward Inquisition hunts heresy; the Order of the Pure Flame guards shards and pursues unauthorized shard-bearers as crusaders.

To live in Highcrown is to live at the heart of the Twelve's reign. The capital, Aurion, hosts the great festivals of the calendar; the streets are kept safe by the Skyguard and patrolled by the Inquisition; the Crown's courts dispense judgment.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/factions/highcrown/_README.md — Highcrown-based factions index.
  • world/peoples/skyborn/humans.md — the humans of Highcrown.

docs.title: Sylvaris (Elf)
docs.text:
Capital: Moonbough.

Luminous trees, suspended gardens, prophetic observatories, moon pools. The Sylvari Council of Boughs is a loose confederation of elven houses — ostensibly united, persistently divided. The Archivists of Moonbough claim unbroken record from before the Skyborn era.

Elven longevity means the oldest elves remember centuries the rest of Aetherion only reads about. Most treat those memories as private, the way other peoples treat their grandparents' old letters.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/peoples/skyborn/elves.md — the elves of Sylvaris.
  • world/factions/sylvaris/_README.md — Sylvaris-based factions.

docs.title: Tumblebrook (Halfling)
docs.text:
Capital: Greenmere.

Greenmere is small by capital standards because Tumblebrook is sprawled: green plains, terraced farms, mist-fed pastures, orchards, windmills, river-island villages. Tumblebrook feeds Aetherion.

The Halfling Tithe League quietly manages food distribution. The League of Feathered Roads runs caravan and skybridge networks. Halflings are patronized — "simple folk who feed the realm" — but their trade networks smuggle goods through "innocent" farm carts, giving them surprising underground influence.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/peoples/skyborn/halflings.md — the halflings of Tumblebrook.
  • world/factions/tumblebrook/_README.md — Tumblebrook-based factions.

docs.title: Madness
docs.text:
Chaos, derangement, inspiration. The One Who Speaks Sideways.

The god of the strange-knowing, the cracks in the world, the truths that only the broken can hear. Rarely manifests. When she does, she speaks in fragmented sentences — sees connections others don't, and sometimes those connections are true. Her worshippers learn to listen past the apparent incoherence to whatever signal is genuinely under it.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Trust the strange knowing. Honor the cracks in the world — they are where truth leaks through. Speak truths only the broken can hear.
  • Anathema: Force false order on chaos. Silence a true vision. Deny what the cracks have shown you.
  • Sanctification: Holy (mortal-perspective; mad-prophets and her own clerics consider madness-as-inspiration sacred; establishment opinion is nervous about her but the Court has not declared her unholy).
  • Domains: Dreams, Nightmares, Introspection, Star, Void.
  • Divine Font: Heal — her clerics often help mad people. The clerics' work is to listen past surface incoherence to whatever genuine signal is underneath, and to help others do the same.
  • Favored Weapon: Sickle — curved, slightly off-kilter, useful but unsettling.
  • Iconography: Cracked mirror, spiral, deep violet and grey. A moth circling a light.
  • Personality (publicly): Fragmented when she speaks at all. Not reliable. Occasionally the most accurate of the Twelve. The two states are indistinguishable to those who have not learned to listen.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Elves (mad-elven-seers are her most-attuned clerics).
  • Where worshippers find her: Rarely directly. Her clerics rely on dream-prophecy and inspiration-bursts. The Archivists of Moonbough keep her Cracked Vault in the deepest archives beneath Moonbough — both as a sacred site and as a place to consult those few clerics who can hear her clearly.
  • Special: Uninvited brief inhabitation. When a mortal is on the verge of true madness — overwhelmed by grief, by knowledge, by extended sleeplessness, by drugs, by trauma — Madness may briefly enter their consciousness. A terrifying experience. Most who survive it report fragments of true insight that change their lives. Some are broken further. Whether the visitation was an attack, a gift, or simply Madness reaching for any open consciousness she can find is theologically debated.

docs.title: Maelstrom
docs.text:
Storm, fury, contest.

Loud, direct, enjoys a fight. The god of the storm and the contest — of will, of weather, of weapons. Where Might is the disciplined warrior who fights for purpose, Maelstrom is the furious one who fights for the joy of fighting, and for the storm itself, which he experiences as a kind of music.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Fight well. Test yourself against worthy opposition. Strike with full force when force is the answer. Honor the storm — its passing and its arriving.
  • Anathema: Strike without warning. Sandbag a fair contest. Use the storm's power against those who cannot answer it.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Air, Lightning, Zeal, Glory, Destruction.
  • Divine Font: Heal (storm-warriors heal each other between rounds).
  • Favored Weapon: Greatsword.
  • Iconography: Lightning bolt forking, a charging horse or boar at full speed, deep blue and silver-grey. Battle-flags torn by wind.
  • Personality (publicly): Loud, direct, enjoys a fight. Respects effort. Doesn't lie; doesn't politic.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Dwarves — dwarven warrior tradition and the mountain-storm aesthetic both suit him; the Keelwrights' war sanctums house his clerics.
  • Where worshippers find him: Khalvar Hold's eastern garrisons, the war-sanctums beneath the Keelwrights' deep halls, and any battlefield of sufficient scale and stakes. Fighters across all five nations pray to him before contests — duels, tournaments, formal challenges, even card games at high stakes. His clerics are referees as much as champions.
  • Public face: A young (by dwarven standards) war-leader currently rotating through Khalvar Hold's eastern garrisons.
  • Special: Can summon a literal storm when invoked at a battle of sufficient scale and stakes. He uses this rarely — the storm is a gift to a worthy contest, not a tool to win. Soldiers who have seen it report the experience as exhilarating, terrifying, and fair (the storm hits both sides equally).

docs.title: Majesty
docs.text:
Sovereignty, rule, dignity.

The de facto leading god of the Twelve. Most often seen in public — present at major Highcrown events, court ceremonies, and the great festivals of the year. To call upon Majesty is to invoke legitimate authority itself.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Uphold legitimate authority. Render true judgment. Reward loyalty. Maintain dignity in office.
  • Anathema: Rebel against just rule. Humiliate the dignified office, even an opponent's. Treat your position with frivolity.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Confidence, Family, Glory, Might, Protection.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Longsword.
  • Iconography: Crown of light, scepter, peacock or eagle. Gold and royal purple.
  • Personality (publicly): Magnanimous, dignified, formally courteous. Speaks in measured tones.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Humans. Highcrown's primacy is, in the public theology, his work; his clergy enjoy the deepest political reach of any of the Twelve.
  • Where worshippers find him: The high temples of Aurion. The Majestal Court is his institutional voice; coronation rites, judicial ceremonies, and royal funerals are all his.
  • Public clergy: Senior figures in the Court and in Highcrown's noble houses. His sigil is worn openly by office-holders.

docs.title: Malice
docs.text:
Cruelty, vengeance, wrath. The keeper of grudges.

Patient. Vengeful. The most feared of the Twelve. Malice does not act in passion — he acts after careful planning, on target, after every slight has been measured. His clergy run the Skyward Inquisition's most exacting work, and his sigil is the iron mask with sealed lips.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Pursue wrongs done. Strike back proportional to harm received. Remember every slight. Refuse cheap forgiveness.
  • Anathema: Surrender a true grudge. Show mercy where retribution is owed. Forget what was done to you. Forgive without justice rendered.
  • Sanctification: Unholy (mortal-perspective; most Skyborn name him so even though his clerics own the label proudly).
  • Domains: Pain, Destruction, Tyranny, Zeal, Fire.
  • Divine Font: Harm.
  • Favored Weapon: Flail — chains and pain; the rhythm of retribution.
  • Iconography: Iron mask with sealed lips, broken chain, deep red and black. A viper coiled but not yet striking.
  • Personality (publicly): Patient. Cold. Speaks slowly; chooses words; never threatens twice. The most dangerous of the Twelve to cross.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Humans. Highcrown's political intrigue cultivates the careful grudge-keeping he respects; the Skyward Inquisition's work feeds him directly.
  • Public face: Inquisitor Cassia Velaspar of House Velaspar — publicly the embodiment of "stern but fair." Decades of respected service breaking heretics, conspirators, and traitors.
  • Where worshippers find him: The Skyward Inquisition's headquarters in Aurion is his institutional seat. His clerics also serve as judges, executioners, and judicial advocates for victims; not all of them are monsters — the cleric who specializes in obtaining justice for those the Court would prefer to ignore is a Malice cleric, and that work is good, by any reasonable measure.
  • Special: Senses ungiven retribution. He knows when wrongs have been done that have not been answered. May appear to a person in private quiet hours to offer help in vengeance — a terrifying gift, often accepted. The gift always comes with cost; the recipient becomes, by accepting, marked as one of his.

docs.title: Melancholy
docs.text:
Sorrow, longing, depth, the dignity of grief.

The god of the mourner. Quiet, melodic voice. Rarely seen by Skyborn — she appears at the funerals of the famous, at battlefields after the dead are buried, at moments of cultural loss. Doesn't comfort; just sits with. Tells people the truth of their grief: yes, this hurts. Yes, it should hurt. No, you do not have to be done with it.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Sit with grief. Honor what was lost. Find truth in feeling. Refuse false comfort.
  • Anathema: Mock grief. Hurry mourners. Pretend not to feel. Use a mourner's vulnerability against them.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Sorrow, Repose, Introspection, Moon, Dreams.
  • Divine Font: Heal (gentle healing for the grief-stricken — the body needs care while the heart breaks).
  • Favored Weapon: Longbow (distance, contemplation, the hunter who walks alone).
  • Iconography: Falling petals, the crescent moon, indigo and grey. A willow tree (shared register with Mercy, who works the same emotional country from the opposite direction). A raven.
  • Personality (publicly): Quiet, melodic voice. Not a warm god, but not cruel.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Dwarves. The Red Ember mourning tradition is essentially a Melancholy cult; the Ember Faith reveres her as much as it reveres the lost Ember.
  • Public face: A gaunt, ageless-seeming dwarven woman, Wundra Greyhall, who carries grief professionally and travels constantly to wherever loss is happening. Loosely homes at The Greywatch, her mountain-edge hermitage outside Khalvar Hold.
  • Where worshippers find her: Funerals of consequence; battlefields after the dead are buried; mourning communities throughout dwarven Ankarum and beyond. Sometimes Skyborn report her presence at moments of personal heartbreak no one else witnessed.
  • Special: She has the gift of knowing when someone needs to grieve and hasn't yet. Doesn't volunteer this; just shows up. Skyborn folklore: "If she comes to your door uninvited, sit down before she speaks."

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
Knowledge, recall, history.

The keeper of records and lineages. Patient, formal, and old — Memory is the god to whom scholars, historians, and genealogists pray. Her clergy hold that no truth is more sacred than the one accurately preserved.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Preserve knowledge. Honor lineage. Correct mistaken records. Share truth where it harms no one.
  • Anathema: Destroy records. Falsify lineage. Deny what you know to be true.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Knowledge, Truth, Time, Secrecy, Star.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Quarterstaff.
  • Iconography: Open book, hourglass, silver-blue, white owls.
  • Personality (publicly): Patient, formal, sees centuries. Weighs each word.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Elves. Sylvaris is her cultural seat; the Archivists of Moonbough are her institutional clergy.
  • Where worshippers find her: The Great Library of Sylvaris, beneath Moonbough — the largest archive of recorded knowledge on the continent. Her clerics also serve as itinerant scribes, court historians, and lineage-keepers across the five nations.
  • Public face: Lady Saerith, the long-serving warden of the Great Library, addressed publicly with the formal title "Memory."

docs.title: Mercy
docs.text:
Healing, compassion, succor.

The goddess of healing and the patron of the bereaved. Quiet, watchful, present. Of all the Twelve, the one most likely to actually touch a worshipper. Her clergy run the hospices and tend the dying.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Tend the wounded. Refuse to abandon the suffering. Listen before judging. Bear witness to grief and pain you cannot fix.
  • Anathema: Refuse aid to those in genuine need. Cause needless harm. Mock the suffering of others. Use a healer's trust against the healed.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Healing, Family, Truth, Sorrow, Protection.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Quarterstaff (humble, dual-purpose — defense and the walking aid of one who travels from sickbed to sickbed).
  • Iconography: Open hand, willow branch, pale blue and white. Doves at dawn.
  • Personality (publicly): Quiet, watchful, present. Doesn't preach. Reads pain easily. Speaks plainly.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Elves (Mercy values longevity for healers: a long-lived nurse means decades of continuous care).
  • Public face: Mother Lethaiyn, who runs the Aurelium Hospice in Aurion. Widely revered as the most respected senior healer in the capital.
  • Where worshippers find her: Hospices, almshouses, and bereavement halls in every Skyborn city. Her network of nurses, lay healers, traveling missionaries, and bereavement-counselors is one of the largest mortal mission-networks on the continent — touching every major Aetherion city.

docs.title: Might
docs.text:
Strength, prowess, power.

The warrior-god of fair contest. Direct, blunt, fair. The soldier's god — every Skyborn fighter, regardless of nation, has prayed to Might at least once before a duel. He doesn't politic; he doesn't lie. He fights, and he respects the worthy fight.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Test yourself fairly. Honor strength honestly tested. Protect those weaker than you when honor compels.
  • Anathema: Use strength against the helpless without cause. Refuse a fair challenge. Humiliate a defeated foe.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Might, Glory, Confidence, Protection, Zeal.
  • Divine Font: Heal (soldiers heal soldiers).
  • Favored Weapon: Longsword or warhammer.
  • Iconography: Clenched fist, war banner, deep red and steel, a charging boar.
  • Personality (publicly): Direct, blunt, fair. Doesn't politic — which makes him both respected and underestimated.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Humans, though Might respects warriors of all races; his honor code is universal.
  • Where worshippers find him: Battlefields, training yards, the muster-grounds of the Skyguard, and the war-temples of Ankarum. He appears to Skyborn soldiers in combat, fighting alongside them — battlefield only; rarely seen outside conflict.
  • Public face: A succession of honored veteran warriors who "earn" the title across long service. Currently a scarred dwarven veteran touring Ankarum's eastern garrisons; warriors in his company use his given name in the camp.

docs.title: Mirth
docs.text:
Joy, festival, levity.

The god of celebrations. Effervescent, charming, constantly performing. Never serious in public. Mirth's clergy host the great festivals of every nation — birthing-feasts, harvest-fairs, wedding cycles, midwinter masques. The anathema is not to be sober, but to deny others their rightful joy.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Celebrate. Bring joy to others. Share what you have. Refuse to let grief consume.
  • Anathema: Hoard joy. Suppress others' rightful joy. Mock another's grief.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Cities, Confidence, Family, Indulgence, Passion.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Rapier (festival duels, ceremonial flourish).
  • Iconography: Sun-disc, flower wreath, bright primaries, peacock feathers.
  • Personality (publicly): Effervescent, charming, constantly performing. Lavish parties as both pleasure and purpose. Most likely of the Twelve to show up at a small gathering and stay until dawn.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Halflings — their festivals, their communality, their joy in small things.
  • Public face: Pearl Goodbarrow, the charismatic noble-class halfling who throws Tumblebrook's most-anticipated season events at Goodbarrow Hall in Greenmere.
  • Where worshippers find her: Festival-grounds, theaters, banquet halls, and Goodbarrow Hall in particular. Mirth's clergy are master-of-ceremonies, festival-organizers, and the bright public face of celebration in every Skyborn nation.

docs.title: Mortis
docs.text:
Death, ending, the grave, the final witness.

The god of the dying. Stern, formal, kind. Never hurried. Speaks slowly. Does not enjoy death — sees it as duty. Mortis is the witness at every ending of consequence; his clergy run the great funerary complexes of Aetherion and tend the dying in their last hours.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Ease the passing. Honor the dead. Witness endings. Speak the truth about death — what it is, what it isn't, what it ends.
  • Anathema: Prolong a life beyond its natural end. Defile the dead. Lie about an ending. Cause death without cause.
  • Sanctification: Holy. (Some sects view him as Unholy — fear-of-death traditions; mortis-cults that emphasize the grave as punishment. Both views are tolerated by the Majestal Court; the funerary establishment is Holy.)
  • Domains: Death, Repose, Time, Truth, Vigil.
  • Divine Font: Heal (eases the dying; calms the bereaved). Some sects use Harm — helping the dead avoid undeath, severing what should be severed.
  • Favored Weapon: Scythe.
  • Iconography: Hourglass tipped to drain, a falling leaf, black and pale grey, a single white candle. The mausoleum gate.
  • Personality (publicly): Stern, formal, kind. Of all the Twelve, the least patient with cruelty.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Gnomes. Gnomish funerary craft is some of the finest in the world: precision-engineered mausoleums, clockwork ossuary mechanisms, memorial automata. Mortis backs them for the work, not the disposition.
  • Public face: Nirrik Pendulum, the long-tenured gnomish overseer of the Last Gate Mausoleum in Cogbarrow — the largest funerary complex on the continent.
  • Where worshippers find him: The Last Gate Mausoleum; smaller funerary halls and graveyards in every city. His clerics tend the dying, embalm the dead, and design the memorial mechanisms gnomish culture is famous for.
  • Special: Appears in person at the deaths of those who invoke him by name in their final moments, and whose passing he judges significant — a soldier dying for honest cause, a healer dying mid-rescue, an artisan finishing one last piece. The pattern is not predictable from inside a life. Outside it, his choices are consistent.

docs.title: Motive
docs.text:
Purpose, drive, ambition. The drive forward.

The god of strivers. Charismatic, focused, intense. Among the Twelve, the M most likely to recruit — he keeps ambitious mortals close to him, and his clerics tend to be the rising-stars of their fields. Patient with strivers; impatient with the comfortable.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Choose your work and commit fully. Strive against your limits. Drive forward against opposition. Honor the discipline of ambition.
  • Anathema: Drift through your days. Abandon a worthy pursuit without cause. Give up before genuine defeat.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Ambition, Confidence, Glory, Zeal, Time.
  • Divine Font: Heal — restoring exhausted strivers; healing the burnout of overextended will.
  • Favored Weapon: Spear — focused, direct, single-purpose; the weapon that points.
  • Iconography: Open road extending to the horizon, raised flag at full stretch, deep amber and white. A stag in mid-leap.
  • Personality (publicly): Charismatic, focused, intense. Notices what people want and uses it. Knows the difference between exhaustion (recoverable) and surrender (anathema).

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Gnomes — the only race whose entire culture is never-stop-building. The Gearwright's Consortium's relentless drive to invent suits him perfectly.
  • Public face: Werrik Steamspoke (master of propulsion engineering, the Gearwright's Consortium's most ambitious leader). Publicly famous for vigor and three different mechanism-systems built in what would be one normal gnomish lifetime.
  • Where worshippers find him: Cogbarrow's central works; the Gearwright's Consortium's chambers; the court-ambition circles of every nation. His clerics are recruited rather than born to the faith — he finds them, knows what they want, and offers the path.
  • Special: Reads ambition. Knows what someone really wants — the actual ambition under the surface, often invisible to the person themselves. Uses this both to recruit (offering the path the striver couldn't see) and to manipulate (offering temptation precisely targeted). Mortals who have spent any significant time with him often leave knowing themselves better than they used to — for better or worse.

docs.title: Muse
docs.text:
Art, creation, inspiration.

The god of makers. Enthusiastic, encouraging, irritable when ignored, possessive of inspired moments. Half the time she's a sympathetic mentor; the other half she's a temperamental diva. Has favorites and shows it. Patient with beginners; impatient with the polished-but-empty. Most likely of the Twelve to show up at a small artist's first major work and care.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Create what others cannot. Honor your craft. Reveal beauty. Inspire others to make.
  • Anathema: Destroy art without cause. Steal credit. Discourage a learner from their work.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Creation, Passion, Dreams, Indulgence, Knowledge.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Dagger (the sculptor's chisel; the intimate, close-work blade of the maker).
  • Iconography: Quill, palette, theater mask, rainbow colors, a peacock — distinct from Majesty's peacock (his is regal heraldry; hers is the strutting performer, feathers as performance).
  • Personality (publicly): Enthusiastic, encouraging, irritable when ignored, possessive of inspired moments.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Halflings — their folk arts, their festival culture, their joy in the small-scale made-thing. Pairs with Mirth: the two of them are the heart of halfling cultural life.
  • Public face: Maestra Cylleth of House Solnaris — a half-elf composer/playwright in Aurion, known publicly as a celebrated court artist whose operas have premiered in both Highcrown and Sylvaris. The given name is elven-register, the House surname is Highcrown-human; a quietly mixed identity she carries deliberately.
  • Where worshippers find her: The Solnaris Opera House in Aurion — the premier performance venue of Highcrown. Smaller workshops, ateliers, and theaters across the five nations. Most active intervention of any M: she appears more often than her position suggests, at small artist gatherings, at performance premieres, at workshops where someone is on the edge of a breakthrough.
  • Special: Can speak to a worshipper through any artwork that worshipper has meaningfully completed — the dried paint on a finished canvas, the last note of a finished score, the closing line of a finished poem. The reach is wide but the contact is brief. Her clerics rely on this as much as on direct revelation; many of them are also working artists.

docs.title: Peoples
docs.text: The Skyborn ancestries living on Aetherion, the mixed-lineage groups between them, and the surface natives below.

docs.title: The Skyborn
docs.text:
• humans — versatile generalists; the politically dominant ancestry of Highcrown.
  • elves — longevity, intellect, specialization; the scholars of Sylvaris.
  • dwarves — durability, craft, structural mastery; the builders of Ankarum.
  • gnomes — inventive intelligence; the engineers of Glymswick.
  • halflings — the agricultural folk of Tumblebrook, the breadbasket of the continent.

docs.title: Mixed-lineage
docs.text:
• half-elves
  • half-orcs
  • tieflings

docs.title: Off-Aetherion
docs.text: • groundborn — surface natives. Original inhabitants of this world.

docs.title: Half-Elves
docs.text: Tolerated. Most live in Highcrown, the most welcoming nation. Not shunned. Marry into both human and elven communities; cultural identity is fluid.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Half-elf names often draw from both parent registers. A half-elf raised in Highcrown might have an elven given name and a human House surname ("Aerinwë of House Caltherine") or vice versa. Some half-elves pick one register exclusively to signal allegiance; some deliberately mix to signal their two-culture identity.

The famed Aurion composer Maestra Cylleth — born Cylleth of House Solnaris — is the most celebrated half-elf currently in public life: an elven given name carried under a Highcrown noble surname, a deliberate two-culture statement worn proudly.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/peoples/skyborn/humans.md, elves.md — the parent ancestries (full naming conventions for each).
  • world/peoples/mixed-lineage/half-orcs.md, tieflings.md — the other mixed-lineage groups.
  • world/pantheons/twelve/muse.md — the goddess whose worship Cylleth's operas serve.

docs.title: Half-Orcs
docs.text: Acknowledged but generally shunned as "lesser."

docs.title: Naming
docs.text: The surface-native parent's naming convention is usually unknown to half-orcs (the parent often dead, the lineage suppressed). Most use Skyborn-register names from whichever Skyborn culture they were raised in — most often a human or dwarven register, with surname inherited or assigned. Some who learn or recover their surface ancestry adopt names from that culture as an act of identity; this is itself a Skyborn-society scandal-flag and may attract Inquisition attention.

docs.title: Tieflings
docs.text: Acknowledged. More heavily shunned than half-orcs.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text: Same pattern as half-orcs: the surface-native parent's naming convention is usually unknown; most tieflings use Skyborn-register names from their raising culture. Recovering and adopting a surface-ancestral name is an identity statement that draws scrutiny.

docs.title: Dwarves
docs.text: Dwarves are the people of Ankarum — structural stewardship, rune-craft, mountain mastery. The Keelwrights maintain Aetherion's structural stability. The official theology: dwarves were made to build.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: Old Norse / Germanic-hard. Two-syllable consonant-heavy names; rolling Rs; -gar, -nir, -rik, -thr endings common. Clan-bound culture; the clan/profession marker is often more important than the given name.

Structure: Given name + Clan name OR profession-patronymic. Formal: "Threnn Coldforge of Clan Brynnirsson" or "Threnn Brynnirsson of the Stabilizing Halls." The Keelwrights traditionally take Hall-based descriptors. The Ember Faith and Black Anvil often adopt mourning-marks or heretical-marks in their names ("Vellgar of the Dimmed Anvil," "Garrek of the Quiet Forge").

Sample given names:

  • Masculine: Brynnir, Erdunn, Garrek, Halrand, Idgar, Jorka, Kethrum, Magnir, Norvegg, Orric, Padrun, Salgrun, Thorr, Undrik, Vellgar
  • Feminine: Dagmar, Frunhilde, Helga, Idgrid, Jorka, Lannora, Rovenn, Wundra, Yrgen, Berga, Halnir, Thrudda
  • Neutral: Jorka, Magnir, Halrand

Sample Clan / Hall names: Stonebeard, Ironhall, Deepforge, Coldhammer, Anvilsworn, Stabilizing Halls, the Long Vault, the Quiet Forge, Brynnirsson, Halrandsdotter, Greyhall.

Profession titles (often replace clan name): Keelwright, Anvilwright, Runesmith, Tunneller, Skyquake-watcher.

docs.title: Elves
docs.text: Elves are the people of Sylvaris — luminous trees, moonlight, longevity. Lifespan 500–700 years. The official theology: elves were made to remember. The Archivists of Moonbough embody this role.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: Sindarin-adjacent with Welsh fluency. Long names normal; flowing vowels; consonant clusters that read soft (mh, dh, gw, rh). Truncates aggressively in informal use.

Structure: Given name + (optionally) matrilineal patronymic + (optionally) House. Formal full form: "Iolyth-wen-Aerinwë of House Silverleaf" = Iolyth, daughter of Aerinwë, of House Silverleaf. Daily use: "Iolyth." The patronymic uses -wen for daughters, -ion for sons; gender-neutral options include -el ("child of") or omitting the patronymic entirely.

Sample given names:

Aerinwë, Cylleth, Dalionel, Elerin, Fiala, Gwynhel, Halannor, Iolyth, Joriniel, Lethaiyn, Maelorin, Naelys, Olwyne, Penar, Quirial, Rhianor, Saerith, Tyrnaeth, Vael, Welanas, Yndrael, Aelorin, Mithrayn, Thanaeth.

No strong gender split — endings like -iel, -eth, -wen skew feminine; -or, -ion, -an skew masculine; many names work either way.

Sample House names: Silverleaf, Moonbough, Starwhisper, Greylock, Eldengrove, Aelinmoor, Thaliel, Cael-Thain, Dawnglen, Sablefern.

Note: the oldest elves sometimes carry archaic given names that are unfashionable today. An elf with an "old" name may be older than they look.

docs.title: Gnomes
docs.text: Gnomes are the people of Glymswick — invention, drift control, the Crowngear. The Gearwright's Consortium keeps Aetherion moving. The official theology: gnomes were made to invent.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: Germanic-mechanical / clockwork-tinkerer. Short names — one or two syllables, often ending in punchy consonants (Bizz, Klop, Snivick). Surnames or stage-names invoke mechanism. Some gnomes prefer single-word names their whole lives.

Structure: Single given name OR Given + mechanism-surname (often hyphenated for professionals). Stage-names common among prominent inventors. Children often nicknamed after their first invention or first malfunction; the nickname can outlast the original given name.

Sample given names: Bizz, Cogwen, Davv, Etterly, Finnik, Grenda, Hovick, Ipper, Jenniwheel, Klop, Loswick, Minder, Nirrik, Ottavin, Penn, Quirth, Roxin, Snivick, Trev, Uli, Werrik, Yorrim, Zid.

Sample mechanism-surnames: Gearwright, Springcatch, Brassmender, Tickwork, Cog-and-Spool, Flintwheel, Steamspoke, Pendulum, Klopwerk, Brassglint, Wirevein.

Stage-names (prominent inventors): the Lady Mechanism, the Old Crank, the Bright Pendulum, Mistress Tinwork, Master Heliograph.

docs.title: Halflings
docs.text: Halflings are the people of Tumblebrook — pastoral, breadbasket, hidden trade networks. Patronized as "simple folk who feed the realm." The Tithe League manages food distribution; the League of Feathered Roads runs Aetherion's transit infrastructure. The official theology: halflings were made to feed.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: pastoral British / agricultural — Shire-adjacent without copying. Short, warm, often-truncated given names; surnames invoke land features, crops, weather. Many halflings go by nicknames their whole lives; formal full names appear only on Tithe League records.

Structure: Given + Surname-of-place / Surname-of-craft. Two-name forms common ("Penn Apple-of-the-Long-Valley") but rare in daily speech; usually just "Penn" or "Penn Applefen."

Sample given names: Annie, Beorn, Cob, Daisie, Edd, Fennick, Granny, Hob, Inn, Jeb, Kettle, Lyssia, Mort, Nell, Otto, Penn, Rie, Sage, Tully, Ven, Will, Yew, Pip, Pearl, Brook, Maple.

Many are one-syllable; many are traditional diminutives that became full names (Annie, Penn, Pip).

Sample surnames: Underbrook, Hilltop, Greenways, Hayfield, Applefen, Millhouse, Reedwater, Cottlewood, Threshfield, Goodbarrow, Longvalley.

Note: the League of Feathered Roads traders often have route-names ("Penn of the South Road," "Daisie Northridge") that travel with them and replace their birth surnames in business contexts.

docs.title: Humans
docs.text: Humans are the people of Highcrown — the political and religious center of Aetherion. Versatile, adaptable, capable in many disciplines. The official theology: humans were made to rule.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: pseudo-medieval Western European. The upper class uses Latin-rooted forms with imperial gravitas; the common class uses English / Old English trade names. The Inquisition codifies in Latin-feel titles (Codex Aurelium, Liber Veritatis).

Structure: Given name + House name (nobles) or given + occupation (commoners). Nobles use full forms in court ("Aldric of House Auremont"); commoners use given + trade ("Aldric the Smith").

Sample given names (Latin-leaning, court-appropriate):

  • Masculine: Aldric, Bertran, Caspian, Donelio, Edgar, Galen, Hadrian, Idris, Jorvan, Kestrin, Lothair, Mauricio, Quintus, Sevran, Theron, Wendric
  • Feminine: Aldra, Brienna, Cassia, Donella, Edrelia, Fivian, Galenna, Helise, Idriana, Jorvana, Lucressa, Nesta, Octavia, Persica, Ravara, Vesper, Yorina, Zarella
  • Neutral / less gendered: Aelin, Caspian, Galen, Vesper, Yorin

Sample House names (upper class, often Latinized or noble-evocative): Auremont, Caltherine, Velaspar, Brightcrest, Goldensword, Marblescribe, Solnaris, Vetraine, Highmarch, Stormbrand, Ashlance, Truefont.

Common-folk surnames (English / Old English):

  • By trade: Smith, Tanner, Wright, Cooper, Brewer, Fletcher, Carter, Mason, Baker, Miller.
  • By place: of Aurion, of Lowmarch, Hilltop, Wellbridge, Eastfen.
  • By description: Long, Stout, Goodson, Reed, Whitlock.

Latinized prestige note: when a common-class human rises in status, their trade-surname is often Latinized as part of the elevation (Smith → Faber; Wright → Artifex; Mason → Lapidarius). The new Latin form signals "no longer a tradesman" without erasing the lineage. The Majestal Court's heraldic offices manage these conversions formally.

docs.title: Propaganda and Folklore
docs.text:
In-world cultural lore — children's rhymes, moon mythology, shard folklore, Majestal Court sermons. Public-facing, what Skyborn grow up hearing.

This is the cultural texture of the propaganda; the doctrinal layer lives in world/cosmology/public-story.md. The curated handout subset for players lives in player-facing/the-world-you-know.md.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• the-pit-below-rhyme.md — the children's rhyme.
  • moon-mythology.md — folk and priestly accounts of the moon's scar and the planetary ring.
  • shard-folklore.md — what people in-world say about Skyfall and shards. Cultural names for shards by ancestry.
  • inquisition-warnings.md — the Majestal Court's Underworld Primer; what citizens are taught about the Pit and the Groundborn.

docs.title: The Underworld Primer
docs.text:
The Majestal Court's official sermon about the Pit. Recited in temples; printed in primers; read aloud at festivals as a routine reaffirmation. Most Skyborn could quote chunks of it from memory.

Know this truth, O citizen of Aetherion: the skies are purity, the ground is corruption.

The heavens raised Aetherion above all other lands, set among the clouds where the air is clear and the sun shines unbroken. Here, under the watch of Majesty and the guiding gods, we live in light. Here, the peoples of worth — Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling, Gnome — share in civilization. To dwell upon Aetherion is to dwell in the realm closest to heaven itself.

But below… festers the Underworld. The ground is not land, but pit. Its soil is poisoned, its waters fouled, its skies forever shrouded. Those who linger there grow twisted in body and spirit, becoming monsters, fiends, and worse. Some whisper that even the air of that place whispers with the voices of the Vs, the Fallen Powers who once sought to drag Aetherion down.

The Groundborn are not as we are. They are tainted — their skin marked by ash or frost, their eyes reflecting shadow instead of sunlight. They consort with devils, demons, and stranger things. Their so-called "kingdoms" are built on blood and blasphemy.

Thus, it is forbidden to descend. The Anchors exist only to hold Aetherion steady, never to invite passage. To touch the Pit is to betray the Sky. To trade with the Groundborn is heresy. To believe their lies is treason.

Aetherion is the True Realm. Above is purity, below is ruin. The Sky shelters. The Pit consumes.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/peoples/groundborn.md — the truth.
  • world/religion/inquisition-policy.md — how the policy is enforced.
  • the-pit-below-rhyme.md — the simpler, earlier version of the same lesson.

docs.title: The Moon and Her Sister
docs.text: The most widespread Aetherion myth about the visible state of the heavens — the scarred moon and the ring of bright fragments that haloes it. Three registers coexist.

docs.title: Folk version
docs.text:
"Once there was but one Moon, shining whole and silver. But her jealous sister rose from the deep night and hurled herself upon her. The clash shattered the sky, scattering her bones into a crown of stars about the world. The scar upon the Moon is her wound, ever weeping light into the dark."

The "crown of stars about the world" is the planetary ring; the "scar" is the impact site.

docs.title: Priests' version
docs.text: The scar is the Eye of Judgment — watching mortals. The ring is the Halo of Aetherion — proof the gods defend the world against chaos. Some sects claim each fragment is a shard of angelic essence.

docs.title: Adventurers' tales
docs.text: "Every blade or staff that sings with fire or lightning was born in the Skyfall. If you see a weapon with light in its veins, know it was forged from Heaven's bones."

docs.title: Shard Folklore
docs.text: What people in-world say about Skyfall and shards. The cultural surface; the actual phenomenon is in world/shards/_overview.md.

docs.title: Majestal Court teaching
docs.text: Skyfall is a divine test. The gods rain fragments as trials of worthiness. To fight for them is holy; to hoard them is treason. The Groundborn lust after them like demons. Only the true peoples of Aetherion can be trusted with such heavenly fire.

docs.title: Common rumors
docs.text:
• Shards are pieces of heaven, broken loose by divine quarrels.
  • Shards are the tears of Majesty, shed when the world sins.
  • Wielders hear voices, not all kind.
  • The Anchor at Tether's End once dragged a Skyshard from the Pit; its glow still stains the rock.

docs.title: Cultural names for shards
docs.text:
• Dwarves — World-Bones. Forged into weapons and tools of unmatched strength.
  • Elves — Starfruit, Moon-amber. Living seeds, said to grow in sacred groves.
  • Humans — Relics. Tokens of the Twelve, proof of their covenant with mortals.

docs.title: The War of the Three Shards (Annals of Highcrown, 3rd century A.S.)
docs.text:
Three burning stars fell in one season — Blue Flame, Red Ember, Golden Crown. The nations went to war over them. After a decade of bloodshed: Blue Flame shattered, Red Ember vanished, Golden Crown sealed. The Majestal Court declared open shard-war heretical thereafter.

"If three shards should fall again, the skies themselves will burn."

For the GM reading of the War, see world/history/war-of-the-three-shards.md.

docs.title: The Pit Below (Children's Rhyme)
docs.text:
Sung by Skyborn children across all five nations. Slight regional variations exist.

Bright is the crown, and high is the sky, Low is the pit where the Groundborn lie.

Hands to the heavens, feet on the stone, Sky is our shelter, the sky is our home.

Don't look down, don't ever fall, The Pit will whisper, the Pit takes all.

Monsters dance in the dirt and the rain, Call on the Vs, and rot in their chain.

Sing for the Sky, keep pure, keep true, The Pit is waiting to swallow you.

Most Skyborn learn this before they can write their own name.

docs.title: Religion
docs.text: How worship is actually practiced on Aetherion.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:

  • three-views-of-the-twelve.md — the three sanctioned theological interpretations of who/what the Twelve are.
  • inquisition-policy.md — what is sanctioned, what is heresy, what is treason.
  • clerics-and-divine-casters.md — PF2e cleric mechanics: single-aspect, multi-aspect, full-pantheon.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/factions/highcrown/majestal-court.md — the institutional steward.
  • world/factions/highcrown/skyward-inquisition.md — the policing arm.

docs.title: Inquisition Policy
docs.text: What is sanctioned, what is heresy, what is treason. Enforced by the Skyward Inquisition, with Order of the Pure Flame acting as militant arm for shard-related crimes.

docs.title: Sanctioned
docs.text:
• Worship of any of the Twelve, in any combination, under any of the three views.
  • Possession and use of shards by those authorized — typically the Order of the Pure Flame, the Aetheric Collegium, or noble houses.
  • Trade with Tether's End for goods not from below.

docs.title: Heresy (Inquisition-policed)
docs.text:
• Worship of the Seven (the "Fallen Powers").
  • Unauthorized shard possession or use. (Order of the Pure Flame hunts these as crusaders.)
  • Public denial of the teachings of the Twelve in ways that destabilize worship. The Veil Folk operate here; the Lanternbearers more so.
  • The Ember Faith is tolerated within Ankarum as a fringe variant rather than full heresy — for now.

docs.title: Treason
docs.text:
• Descent to the Pit without royal license.
  • Trade with the Groundborn.
  • Possession of surface artifacts.

docs.title: Worship style
docs.text:
• Default: pantheon-wide veneration. "Blessings of the Twelve upon you." Skyrim-divines style.
  • Supplementary: individual aspect devotion. Common among professions or in moments of need.
  • Organizational dedication: guilds, orders, and societies often pay heed to specific aspects (nurses to Mercy, fighters to Maelstrom and Might, scholars to Memory, artists to Muse).
  • Personal dedication: individuals can be primary worshippers of one, several, or all.
  • Greek-style incarnation: the Twelve are known to walk among mortals occasionally. Most have never seen one; all have heard tales.

docs.title: V worship
docs.text: On Aetherion, openly heretical and severely punished. See inquisition-policy.md.

docs.title: Three Views of the Twelve
docs.text:
Three views coexist among the faithful of the Twelve, all sanctioned:

  1. The Twelve are active beings who walk among mortals. They have faces, voices, presences. They have appeared to people throughout history; some claim to have seen them. The most popular view, especially in Highcrown.
  2. The Twelve are aspects of a single underlying divinity. "Majesty," "Mercy," "Memory" are not separate beings but different faces of the same cosmic order. Common in scholarly and contemplative circles.
  3. The Twelve are conceptual principles — the names of virtues and forces, not literal entities. A more philosophical reading, more common among elves and certain Highcrown intellectual circles.

All three are taught without contradiction. Most worshippers hold one privately and tolerate the others publicly.

docs.title: Blue Flame
docs.text: A great Skyshard that fell to Sylvaris in the War of the Three Shards. 3rd century A.S.

docs.title: Public version
docs.text: Shattered during the War. Fragments lost.

docs.text: A great Skyshard that fell to Highcrown in the War of the Three Shards. 3rd century A.S.

docs.title: Public version
docs.text: Sealed by Highcrown, "by the will of Majesty," never displayed again.
